resume:
Marcus Tate - Staff Product Designer

Summary: 11 years of product design, last 4 focused on enterprise SaaS workflow tools.

Experience:
2021 - present  Helio (Series D HR-tech) - Staff Product Designer, Onboarding & Lifecycle
  - Situation: company's new-hire onboarding flow had a 31% drop-off between offer-accept and day-1 readiness, costing Helio ~$4M/yr in delayed time-to-productivity.
  - Task: redesign the candidate-to-employee transition as a single product surface across web and mobile.
  - Action: led discovery with 22 customer admins and 47 new hires across 6 enterprise accounts; mapped 14 hand-off points between recruiting, IT, and HRIS; shipped a unified "first 30 days" surface in 5 iterative releases.
  - Result: reduced drop-off from 31% to 12% within two quarters; admin-side ticket volume on onboarding dropped 44%; surface became a reference design adopted by 3 sibling product lines.

2018 - 2021  Stripe-adjacent fintech - Senior Product Designer
  - S: dispute-management console had 9.3 NPS and a 22-min average task time for the most common dispute path.
  - T: redesign the console with the operations team's actual workflow in mind.
  - A: shadowed 6 ops analysts for two full weeks; built a workflow-state model; redesigned around a queue-based primary nav.
  - R: average task time on the top-3 paths dropped to 9 min; NPS rose to 41; the workflow-state model was reused for two adjacent consoles.

2014 - 2018  Design agency - Product Designer (multiple clients)
  - S/T/A/R bullets omitted for space; portfolio available on request.

Education: BFA Industrial Design, RISD, 2014.

Tools: Figma, FigJam, Maze, Dovetail, Lyssna, Linear, Notion.
jd:
Role: Staff Product Designer - Workflow Platform

We are a Series C B2B SaaS company building workflow software for mid-market operations teams. We are hiring a Staff designer to anchor the platform's core canvas - the surface where every customer spends 60%+ of their day.

Responsibilities: own end-to-end design for the canvas surface and adjacent workflow primitives; partner with two product managers and one design engineer; mentor three mid-level designers without formal management responsibility; set the design quality bar across the platform.

We care a lot about: depth in workflow / multi-step / state-heavy product design (not just landing pages or marketing surfaces); a portfolio that shows shipped, lived-in work and clear cause-and-effect between design decisions and outcomes; an ability to articulate tradeoffs (especially against engineering cost and against research signal); generous and direct mentorship behavior.

Must-haves: 8+ years of product design; at least one shipped enterprise SaaS surface used daily by 1k+ DAUs; comfort presenting work to a CPO-level audience; experience navigating ambiguity in early-stage product bets.

history.q: Marcus, your resume calls out the Helio onboarding redesign — the 31% to 12% drop-off result. Can you walk me through one of the design decisions in that project that was genuinely hard, where you had to choose between two real options?
That's helpful. Now, you mentioned mapping 14 hand-off points between recruiting, IT, and HRIS. Of those 14, were any of them ones you actively chose not to redesign — and if so, why?

Got it. Last one on Helio: the 44% drop in admin-side onboarding tickets — can you share what the absolute ticket volume was before and after, and how you attributed the drop specifically to your redesign rather than to other concurrent changes?

candidate_last_answer: Yeah, fair question. So the situation: admin-side onboarding tickets were running at roughly 1,800 per month across our top 40 enterprise accounts before the redesign — that's a number I can share because it was in our public Q3 earnings narrative as a customer-success metric. After two quarters, they were running around 1,000 per month, so the 44% is (1800 - 1000)/1800. I do have to be careful here — the absolute monthly counts at the per-customer level are under NDA from those specific enterprise accounts, so I can't share segmentation by account. On attribution: we ran the redesign as a phased rollout — accounts in cohort A got the new surface in March, cohort B in May, cohort C in July. The drop showed up in each cohort within four to six weeks of their specific cutover, and the slope matched the rollout calendar pretty cleanly. We also held cohort D unchanged through Q2 as a control, and their ticket rate stayed flat at ~450/month. So the action was a phased cohort rollout with one held-out control, and the result was both the directional drop and a control-arm null that gave us confidence the redesign was the dominant cause, not a concurrent CS-team policy change that shipped in April.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: cohort D unchanged through Q2 as a control
expected-yield: 由Staff级'设计师主导A/B/控制组rollout'这一较强ownership信号，转向探查他在跨职能（PM、Eng、CS）中如何获得对照组hold-out的同意，验证Staff级横向领导力
